metadata.summary: Framework for identifying, validating, and operationalizing a single metric that captures core customer value and aligns all teams — includes selection process and validation tests.

content.title: North Star Metric
content.summary: A framework for identifying, validating, and operationalizing a single metric that captures the core value a product delivers to customers — predicts long-term revenue, aligns all teams, and serves as the root of the OKR tree. Includes a 4-step selection process, validation test, real-product examples, and input-metrics tree pattern.

why.p: Teams tracking 50 metrics optimize in different directions. A North Star Metric (NSM) forces agreement on what "customer value delivered" means, makes trade-offs explicit, and converts disagreements about priorities into measurable hypotheses. Revenue and page views are explicitly disqualified — they measure company extraction or activity, not value delivery.

list.item: Teams are misaligned on what success looks like day-to-day.
Marketing optimizes for clicks, product for features, sales for revenue — no shared growth lever.
Setting up OKRs and need a root metric that team-level key results should roll up to.
Scaling growth investment and need to validate that acquisition spend improves the right outcome.
Pre-launch with no usage data — the NSM requires retention/revenue correlation to validate.
Very early (solo, pre-PMF) stage — pick one proxy metric and iterate; formal NSM process adds overhead before it helps.
When the product serves two fundamentally different user types with different value definitions — a single NSM will be dishonest for one group.

section.name: definition-and-characteristics

rule.statement: The North Star Metric must measure customer value delivered, not company value extracted; it must be a leading indicator of revenue, not revenue itself.
rule.rationale: Revenue as NSM causes teams to optimize for short-term extraction — discounts, forced upgrades, retention walls — at the expense of actual value delivery. When the NSM rises because customers are getting more value, revenue follows; when revenue is chased directly, product quality declines.

section.p: Five characteristics of a valid NSM: measures customer value (not company value), is a leading indicator of revenue, reflects the product vision, is actionable (specific teams can move it), is understandable by everyone in one sentence.

section.name: disqualified-metrics
section.p: Revenue: lags value delivery and causes extraction optimization. Sign-ups: no value exchange has occurred yet. Page views: activity without proven value. DAU/MAU alone: presence without value delivery. These are inputs or lagging outcomes, not NSM candidates.

Real NSM examples by product: Airbnb — nights booked; Spotify — time spent listening; Slack — daily active users × messages sent; Uber — rides completed; Netflix — hours watched; Shopify — gross merchandise volume.

rule.statement: Run the four-step NSM selection: identify core value delivered, find the action that proves customers received that value, find the metric measuring that action, validate that improving the metric improves long-term revenue.
rule.rationale: Without the validation step (step 4), teams adopt a plausible-sounding NSM that does not actually predict business outcomes. The validation test requires empirical correlation analysis between the candidate metric and retention or revenue growth.

section.name: value-equation
section.p: NSM = (action that delivers value) × frequency × number of users. This formula ensures the NSM captures breadth (users), depth (frequency), and quality (value action).

example.note: Project management tool. Core value: helping teams complete work efficiently. NSM: weekly tasks completed per active team. Why: shows value delivery (work getting done), combines engagement with value, correlates with retention and revenue. Input metrics: number of active teams (growth), tasks created per team (adoption), task completion rate (product quality).

E-learning platform. NSM: lessons completed (not hours watched). Why lessons, not hours: watching is not learning; completion signals value delivery. Input metrics: new enrollments, enrollment-to-start rate, lesson completion rate, re-engagement rate (returning students).

Freelance marketplace. NSM: projects completed with positive review. Why: shows successful value exchange for both sides; predicts repeat usage from both clients and freelancers. Input metrics: active freelancers, jobs posted, job-to-hire rate, delivery rate, satisfaction rate.

rule.statement: Run the NSM validation test quarterly: confirm that when the NSM goes up, retention goes up and revenue eventually follows; confirm that when the NSM goes down, churn increases.
rule.rationale: Products evolve. An NSM that was valid at 1,000 users may become a vanity metric at 100,000 users if the product has shifted. Quarterly validation prevents teams from optimizing a stale metric for months before noticing the divergence.

section.name: nsm-and-okrs
section.p: Relationship: NSM is what you optimize for — it rarely changes (quarterly at most). OKRs are quarterly goals that move the NSM. Structure: NSM at top → company-level objective → team-level key results (each owned by one team) → input metrics feeding into the NSM. Each team's KRs should be traceable to a specific NSM input metric.

list.item: List the product's core value in one sentence.
Brainstorm 5-10 candidate metrics that could represent that value.
Test each candidate: Does it measure customer value? Is it a leading indicator? Is it actionable? Can it be explained in one sentence?
Validate empirically: run correlation analysis between each candidate and retention/revenue growth.
Choose one. Get leadership buy-in before proceeding.
Write the exact calculation formula in a shared document — leave no ambiguity about what counts.
Set up tracking so the metric updates at minimum weekly.
Create a dashboard visible to all teams.
Identify 3-5 input metrics and assign one team owner per input metric.
Include NSM in weekly leadership reviews.
Set quarterly NSM targets and align OKRs to them.
Run the validation test quarterly (retention and revenue correlation).

antipattern.description: Choosing multiple North Star metrics ("we have two NSMs").
antipattern.reason: Two NSMs produce the same misalignment problem the NSM was designed to solve. Teams trade off between them differently; there is no single shared success definition. Use input metrics for team-level targets, not additional NSMs.

antipattern.description: NSM that requires a paragraph to explain.
antipattern.reason: If the entire company cannot understand the metric from a one-sentence description, it will not align behavior. Simplify until it passes the "explain to a new hire in 10 seconds" test.

antipattern.description: Setting the NSM and not reviewing the correlation for more than a quarter.
antipattern.reason: Products evolve; metrics that were predictive at one stage become vanity metrics at the next. A stale NSM silently misaligns the company for months before anyone notices.

template.purpose: North Star Metric definition card: metric, why, current state, input metrics, dashboard link.
